seoTitle: YXC Technical Support | Crystal Application Notes | BeiLuo
seoDescription: Technical support resources for YXC crystals and oscillators including application notes, selection guides, PCB layout recommendations, and troubleshooting assistance.

faqs.question: Where can I find YXC product documentation?
faqs.answer: Product datasheets, application notes, and other technical documentation are available on our website. Contact BeiLuo technical support for additional resources or specific questions.

faqs.question: How do I get technical support for my YXC design?
faqs.answer: BeiLuo provides comprehensive technical support for YXC products including design review, troubleshooting assistance, and application guidance. Contact our FAE team through the support portal or email.

faqs.question: Can I get samples for prototyping?
faqs.answer: Yes, samples are available for most YXC products. Contact BeiLuo sales or use our sample request form. Samples typically ship within 1-2 business days.

articles.title: Crystal Oscillator Selection Guide
articles.description: Comprehensive guide to selecting the right crystal or oscillator for your application, covering frequency stability, package selection, load capacitance, and key specifications.
articles.summary: This comprehensive guide covers crystal vs oscillator selection, key parameters like frequency stability and load capacitance, package size considerations, and practical selection criteria for various applications.
articles.content: Selecting the right crystal or oscillator is critical for ensuring reliable system operation. This guide covers the key parameters and considerations for choosing the optimal timing component for your application. Crystal resonators (passive) require external oscillator circuitry in your MCU or dedicated oscillator IC. They offer lower cost and power but require careful circuit design. Crystal oscillators (active) contain the complete oscillator circuit and provide a clock output directly. They are easier to use but consume more power and cost more. Frequency tolerance indicates accuracy at 25C. Frequency stability specifies variation over temperature range. Load capacitance (CL) is the capacitance the crystal sees in the circuit - critical for frequency accuracy. ESR (Equivalent Series Resistance) affects start-up and power consumption. Drive level must not exceed maximum ratings to avoid damage. Common SMD crystal packages: 7.0x5.0mm (legacy), 5.0x3.2mm (industrial), 3.2x2.5mm (general purpose), 2.5x2.0mm (compact), 2.0x1.6mm (ultra-compact), 1.6x1.2mm (wearable). Smaller packages have higher ESR and tighter handling requirements. The total load capacitance CL = (C1 x C2) / (C1 + C2) + Cstray, where Cstray is typically 3-5pF. C1 and C2 are the external capacitors. Incorrect CL causes frequency error. Use the crystal's specified CL for best accuracy. Proper crystal selection requires balancing frequency accuracy, package size, power consumption, and cost. When in doubt, contact YXC technical support for application-specific recommendations.

faq.question: What is the difference between AT-cut and tuning fork crystals?
faq.answer: AT-cut crystals operate in MHz range (1-200MHz+) using thickness-shear vibration mode, offering excellent frequency stability. Tuning fork crystals operate at 32.768kHz using flexural mode, designed specifically for low-power RTC applications. They are not interchangeable.

faq.question: How do I calculate the frequency error from wrong load capacitance?
faq.answer: Frequency error is approximately (CL_actual - CL_specified) x pullability. Typical pullability is 15-20ppm/pF. For example, using 10pF load when 8pF is specified causes approximately +30ppm error (faster frequency).

faq.question: Can I use a crystal rated for -20C to +70C in an automotive application?
faq.answer: No, automotive applications require crystals rated for -40C to +125C (AEC-Q200 Grade 1). Standard crystals will not meet reliability and performance requirements across the automotive temperature range.

faq.question: What causes crystal start-up failure?
faq.answer: Common causes: 1) Excessive load capacitance, 2) Insufficient drive from oscillator circuit, 3) High ESR crystal with weak driver, 4) PCB contamination or moisture, 5) Mechanical damage from shock. Check with an oscilloscope to verify oscillation.

faeInsights.summary: The most common mistake in crystal selection is not matching the load capacitance to the MCU requirements. Always check your microcontroller datasheet for recommended crystal specifications and calculate the exact load capacitor values needed.
keyTakeaways: Choose package size based on assembly capability

customerCases.feedback: Following the selection guide helped us choose the right crystal with proper load capacitance, eliminating timing drift issues we experienced with our previous design.

articles.title: Crystal PCB Layout Best Practices
articles.description: Essential PCB layout guidelines for crystal and oscillator circuits to ensure reliable operation, minimize EMI, and optimize performance.
articles.summary: Learn essential PCB layout techniques for crystal circuits including trace routing, ground plane design, guard ring implementation, and EMI reduction strategies for reliable oscillator operation.
articles.content: Proper PCB layout is crucial for crystal oscillator performance. Poor layout can cause start-up issues, frequency instability, excessive EMI, and sensitivity to noise. Following these guidelines ensures optimal operation. Keep crystal traces as short as possible - ideally under 10mm, maximum 20mm. Route traces symmetrically from MCU to crystal. Avoid vias on crystal traces when possible. Keep traces on same layer if feasible. Do not route high-speed signals near crystal traces. Place a solid ground plane under the crystal area. Implement a guard ring around crystal pads connected to ground. Keep ground plane continuous under crystal - avoid splits. Connect guard ring to ground with multiple vias. Place crystal as close as possible to MCU oscillator pins. Place load capacitors immediately adjacent to crystal. Keep other components away from crystal area. Orient crystal to minimize trace length. Minimize loop area formed by crystal and traces. Use ground stitching vias around crystal perimeter. Consider shielding can for sensitive applications. Keep crystal away from RF circuits and antennas. Following these layout guidelines prevents most crystal-related issues. For critical applications, consider using YXC's layout review service before PCB fabrication.

faq.question: How close should the crystal be to the MCU?
faq.answer: Ideally within 10mm (0.4 inch). Maximum recommended distance is 20mm. Longer traces increase parasitic capacitance and susceptibility to noise, potentially causing start-up issues or frequency errors.

faq.question: Should I use a ground plane under the crystal?
faq.answer: Yes, always use a solid ground plane under the crystal and its traces. This provides return paths, reduces EMI, and improves noise immunity. Avoid splitting the ground plane under the crystal area.

faq.question: Can I route other signals under the crystal on inner layers?
faq.answer: Avoid routing signals under the crystal on any layer. The crystal area should be kept clear on all layers when possible. If unavoidable, only route DC or low-frequency signals, never high-speed or switching signals.

faq.question: What is a guard ring and why is it important?
faq.answer: A guard ring is a grounded trace surrounding the crystal pads. It isolates the crystal from adjacent signals, reduces EMI coupling, and provides a return path for stray fields. Connect the guard ring to ground with multiple vias.

faeInsights.summary: The guard ring is the most overlooked aspect of crystal layout. A proper guard ring can reduce EMI by 10-20dB and prevent many field issues. Always include it in your crystal layout.
keyTakeaways: Place load caps adjacent to crystal

customerCases.feedback: Implementing the guard ring and proper ground plane design eliminated intermittent clock failures we were experiencing in our medical device.

articles.title: Crystal Oscillator Troubleshooting Guide
articles.description: Systematic troubleshooting approach for common crystal oscillator problems including start-up failure, frequency error, and intermittent operation.
articles.summary: Systematic approach to diagnosing and resolving crystal oscillator issues including start-up failures, frequency errors, and intermittent operation with measurement techniques and solutions.
articles.content: Crystal oscillator problems can be challenging to diagnose. This guide provides a systematic approach to identify and resolve common issues including start-up failure, frequency error, and intermittent operation. First, verify power supply is stable and within specification. Check that load capacitors are correct value and properly soldered. Measure ESR if possible - high ESR can prevent start-up. Verify crystal is not damaged from mechanical shock. Check for PCB contamination or moisture. Measure actual frequency with precision counter. Calculate expected error from load capacitance mismatch. Check for temperature effects if error varies with temperature. Verify crystal specifications match application requirements. Consider aging effects in long-term operation. Intermittent issues often indicate marginal start-up conditions. Check for cold solder joints on crystal or capacitors. Verify mechanical stability - shock or vibration can cause intermittent failure. Look for temperature-dependent issues. Check for power supply noise or transients. Use high-impedance probe (x10) with short ground spring to minimize loading. Measure at MCU pin, not crystal pin, to avoid loading effects. Use spectrum analyzer to check for spurious oscillations. Monitor with oscilloscope to verify stable amplitude. Most crystal issues can be resolved through systematic diagnosis. Document all measurements and changes for future reference. Contact YXC support for persistent problems.

faq.question: My crystal doesn't start oscillating. What should I check first?
faq.answer: Check in this order: 1) Power supply stability, 2) Correct load capacitor values, 3) Crystal ESR within spec, 4) PCB layout (trace length, ground plane), 5) Solder joint quality, 6) Crystal damage. Use oscilloscope to verify if any oscillation is occurring.

faq.question: Why does my crystal work on the bench but fail in production?
faq.answer: Common causes: 1) Component tolerance variations, 2) PCB manufacturing variations affecting parasitics, 3) Temperature differences, 4) Mechanical stress from assembly, 5) Soldering temperature damage. Design with margin for these variations.

faq.question: Can I use a scope probe to measure at the crystal pins?
faq.answer: Avoid probing directly at crystal pins as probe capacitance (10-15pF) can stop oscillation or change frequency. Instead, measure at the MCU oscillator pin or use an active FET probe with less than 1pF loading. If you must probe at crystal, use x10 probe with minimal ground connection.

faq.question: What does 'negative resistance' mean in crystal circuits?
faq.answer: Negative resistance is the effective resistance the oscillator circuit presents to the crystal. It must be greater than the crystal's ESR (typically 3-5x) for reliable start-up. Insufficient negative resistance causes start-up failure or marginal operation.

faeInsights.summary: 80% of crystal issues are caused by incorrect load capacitance or poor PCB layout. Always verify these first before suspecting a defective crystal. Use the troubleshooting flowchart for systematic diagnosis.

customerCases.feedback: The troubleshooting guide helped us identify that our load capacitors were 50% too large, causing frequency error and occasional start-up failures.
